title: Carved walnut condiment spoon
subtitle: Small seasoned-walnut spoon for salt, spice, and loose-leaf tea
excerpt: A small condiment spoon in seasoned walnut. Oval bowl, short handle, boarded-butter finish. Walnut carves well from seasoned stock for small treen; the iron-stain note and allergen declaration are part of the project.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on seasoned treen. Beeton's Book of Household Management on condiment-spoon forms.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at a stable moisture content (8 to 12 percent for indoor pieces). Harder to carve than green wood but dimensionally stable.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.
A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the bowl of a spoon. The small variant suits tea and condiment spoon bowls.

content.text: A condiment spoon is 12 to 14 cm long with a small oval bowl. It serves loose-leaf tea, flaky salt, or ground spices. Walnut is a good choice for small
seasoned

treen: the grain is fine, the chocolate-brown tone suits a kitchen display, and the finished surface is very smooth under the hook knife. Two notes before starting: walnut reacts with iron tools, producing a black stain at the cut surface. Wipe the tool with citric acid solution or vinegar if the surface discolours. And walnut is a tree-nut allergen; declare this clearly if the spoon is a gift.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
Blank

: 14 cm long, 4 cm wide, 2.5 cm deep in seasoned walnut. A short off-cut from a furniture board is enough. The moisture content should be below 12 percent. Moisture meter check: probe the end-grain after an hour in the workshop; a reading above 14 percent means the piece needs more drying before carving.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
; a small
hook knife
; a leather strop; board butter. No axe for a small seasoned blank.
Method
Draw the spoon outline: 8 cm handle tapering from 18 mm to 12 mm; 6 cm oval bowl 30 mm wide at the widest point. Mark both faces.
Shape the handle with
push cuts
. Seasoned walnut is harder than green sycamore; the cuts require more forearm pressure. Keep the edge sharp and strop after every 8 to 10 minutes of work.
Shape the outside of the bowl. The bowl exterior is a convex curve; push cuts from the bowl shoulder toward the bowl tip. Use
thumb-pivot cuts
at the shoulder transition where the handle meets the bowl.

Hollow the bowl with the small hook knife. Work from the center outward in overlapping arcs. Target: 5 mm deep, 3 mm wall thickness. Seasoned walnut resists the hook knife more than green wood; take thinner passes and strop the
hook knife
more frequently.
Leave the bowl interior as cut if the surface is clean. Smooth the handle and outside of the bowl through 120 and 180 grit sandpaper along the grain.
Finishing

Board butter is the correct finish for this spoon: a beeswax-and-mineral-oil paste that conditions the surface without curing. Apply with a soft cloth, let stand for twenty minutes, buff off. Re-apply monthly when the spoon is in daily use.
Allergen note

Walnut is a tree nut. A walnut-carved condiment spoon will deposit trace walnut allergens into any food it touches. Label clearly if making for someone with a nut allergy; for that use case, substitute sycamore or birch.
Variations
Sycamore instead of walnut: food-safe, pale, no allergen issue. The grain is finer and easier to smooth with the hook knife.
Longer handle for a tea caddy: extend the handle to 12 cm. Proportionally heavier.
Left-handed bowl: the bowl taper is symmetrical; no change needed.
Care
Rinse under warm water after use, towel-dry immediately. Never dishwasher. The board-butter finish re-conditions on contact with water then oil; re-buff with fresh board butter monthly.

items.symptom: Black stain on the cut surface
items.cause: Walnut reacting with iron in the tool steel
items.fix: Wipe the blade and the cut surface with citric acid solution (half a teaspoon in a cup of water) or white vinegar. The stain lifts from the surface; deeper staining from old dull tools goes deeper into the grain.

items.symptom: Hook knife skates on the seasoned walnut surface
items.cause: Edge not sharp enough for hard seasoned wood
items.fix: Strop the hook knife before each session with seasoned hardwood. A strop that maintains a green-wood hook knife needs two extra passes per side for hard seasoned stock.

items.symptom: Bowl interior has a rough texture
items.cause: The grain direction in the bowl reversed mid-bowl
items.fix: Work the bowl in two halves: from the bowl center toward the shoulder (against the taper) and from the center toward the tip (with the taper). The grain direction reverses at the center of the bowl floor.
